1: Moses goes and speaks these words to all Israel — the conclusion of the great covenantal address. The faithful mediator completes his charge: every word commissioned to him has been spoken. In Christ: "I have glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work that you gave me to do" (John 17:4); "It is finished" (John 19:30). The pattern of the faithful mediator who completes his commission reaches its ultimate expression in Christ. Theme.
2: "I am a hundred and twenty years old today. I am no longer able to lead you out and bring you in." Moses reaches his limit. The human mediator — however faithful — is finite. Christ knows no such limit: "he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them" (Hebrews 7:25). The replacement of the limited Moses by the unlimited Christ is the movement from the old covenant to the new. Theme.
3: "The LORD your God himself will cross over before you... Joshua will lead you across." YHWH goes first as the divine warrior; Joshua follows as the human instrument. In Christ, the two roles are united: he is both the divine actor (Emmanuel, God with us) and the human representative who crosses through death and resurrection. "He is the pioneer and perfecter of faith" (Hebrews 12:2) — the forerunner who goes before his people through the ultimate crossing. Theme.
4: YHWH will deal with the Canaanite nations as he dealt with Sihon and Og. Past victories ground future confidence. The resurrection is the past victory that grounds all future confidence for the church: "Since Christ was raised from the dead, he cannot die again; death no longer has mastery over him" (Romans 6:9). The Sihon and Og victories are the type; the resurrection is the antitype from which all Christian confidence flows. Theme.
5: "The LORD will hand them over to you, and you must deal with them according to all the commands I have given you." Divine initiative and human responsibility operate together. The NT parallel: "work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure" (Philippians 2:12–13). YHWH gives the victory; Israel must act. Christ secures salvation; believers must respond. The two-agency pattern pervades both covenants. Theme.
6: "Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid or terrified by them — the LORD your God goes with you. He will never leave you or abandon you." This promise is directly cited in Hebrews 13:5–6: "For he has said, 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.' So we can confidently say, 'The Lord is my helper; I will not fear.'" The assurance given to Israel before crossing the Jordan becomes the assurance given to every believer in Christ. Direct NT citation (Hebrews 13:5).
7: Moses commissions Joshua publicly: "Be strong and courageous, for you are the one who will lead this people into the land the LORD swore to give their ancestors." Joshua commissioned to lead the people into the rest that Moses could not give. Hebrews 4:8–9 makes the Joshua-Christ typology explicit: "if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on" — the rest Joshua gave was incomplete, pointing to the Sabbath rest that Christ provides. Direct type (Hebrews 4:8–9).
8: "The LORD himself goes before you. He will be with you; he will never leave you or abandon you. Do not be afraid or discouraged." The doubled assurance — to Joshua, then to all Israel. Hebrews 13:5 cites this promise as the ground for the believer's contentment: God himself has promised permanent presence. Jesus echoes it at the Great Commission: "I am with you always, to the end of the age" (Matthew 28:20). The assurance given to the covenant people entering the promised land is the assurance of God's permanent presence in Christ. Direct NT citation (Hebrews 13:5; Matthew 28:20).
9: Moses writes the law in a book and hands it to the Levitical priests. The written Torah — the externally mediated covenant document. The written law on scroll contrasts with the new covenant's inward writing: "not written with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Corinthians 3:3). Moses writes the law; the Spirit writes the law on the heart. The scroll is the shadow; the Spirit-written heart is the fulfillment. Shadow.
10: Every seventh year at the Feast of Booths, read the law publicly. The public covenant renewal reading during the sabbatical year — the time of debt release combined with the festival of joy. Both the debt-release and the joy-festival are fulfilled in Christ: the debt canceled at the cross (Colossians 2:13–14), the feast of joy in the Lord's presence. The Deuteronomic public reading is the prototype of the church's Scripture reading: "Devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture" (1 Timothy 4:13). Theme.
11: "When all Israel comes to appear before the LORD your God at the place he chooses, you must read this law aloud before all Israel in their hearing." The comprehensive public hearing — the entire assembly must hear the covenant word. In the new covenant assembly, the word of God is central: "Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom" (Colossians 3:16). The Deuteronomic public hearing is the template for the church's life under the word. Theme.
12: "Gather the entire people — men, women, children, and the foreigners living in your towns — so they can hear, learn to fear the LORD your God." The comprehensive assembly includes men, women, children, and foreigners. In Christ, the comprehensive assembly is extended to every tribe, tongue, and nation: "you are all one in Christ Jesus" (Galatians 3:28). The all-inclusive assembly that Moses commands becomes the all-inclusive body of Christ. Theme.
13: "The children who do not yet know the law must hear it and learn to fear the LORD your God throughout all the time you live in the land." Intergenerational transmission — covenant knowledge must pass to each new generation. The NT carries this responsibility forward: "bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). In Christ, the transmission is not only of a written code but of a living relationship — "what we have heard... we proclaim also to you, so that you too may have fellowship with us; and indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son Jesus Christ" (1 John 1:1–3). Theme.
14: "The time of your death is drawing near." YHWH tells Moses his death is near. Moses approaches the divine presence for the final commissioning before his death. The mediator who must die and be succeeded: "the former priests were many in number, because they were prevented by death from continuing in office, but he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever" (Hebrews 7:23–24). Moses' death marks the limit of the old covenant mediator; Christ's indestructible life marks the limitlessness of the new. Direct type (Heb 7).
15: YHWH appears at the tent in a pillar of cloud. The theophanic cloud — YHWH's presence in visible, non-image form. The cloud of divine presence recurs at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:5: "a bright cloud overshadowed them"), the ascension (Acts 1:9: "he was taken up... a cloud took him out of their sight"), and the return (Revelation 1:7: "he is coming with clouds"). The cloud is the consistent marker of divine presence whose final appearance is the return of Christ. Theme.
16: YHWH warns: after Moses' death, Israel will chase foreign gods and break the covenant. The divine foreknowledge of Israel's apostasy. The foreknown failure is the occasion for the promised remedy: Jeremiah 31:31–34 — "Not like the covenant I made with their fathers... my covenant that they broke... I will make a new covenant." The predicted covenant-breaking of Deuteronomy 31:16 is the negative space that the new covenant fills. In Christ, the new covenant that addresses this failure is enacted: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood" (Luke 22:20). Theme.
17: "My anger will blaze against them that day, and I will abandon them and hide my face from them." The hiding of the divine face (hester panim) — the withdrawal of God's favorable presence — is the deepest dimension of covenant curse. Christ experiences this desolation at the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1). The hidden face of God that Israel would experience in exile, Christ experienced in abandonment at Calvary — absorbing the covenant curse so that those in him need not face permanent divine abandonment. Shadow fulfilled in Christ's cross-desolation.
18: "I will certainly conceal my face that day because of all the evil they have done — they have turned to other gods." The concealed face of God as the consequence of idolatry. In Christ, the concealed face becomes the revealed face: "the light of the knowledge of God's glory displayed in the face of Christ" (2 Corinthians 4:6). The face hidden in divine wrath is unveiled in the Son. What was turned away from the covenant-breaker is turned toward those in Christ. Direct contrast and fulfillment.
19: "Now write down this song and teach it to the Israelites... so that this song will serve as a witness for me against them." The song of Moses — the great covenant witness-poem of chapter 32. In Revelation 15:3–4, the redeemed standing before the throne sing "the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb," praising YHWH for his justice and holiness. The song that testified against covenant-breaking Israel becomes, in the new creation, the song of the redeemed who have been freed from the covenant curse by the Lamb. Direct NT connection (Revelation 15:3–4).
20: YHWH predicts: when they prosper in the land of milk and honey, they will turn to other gods and break the covenant. The pattern of prosperity-leading-to-apostasy is predicted before it happens — the entire subsequent history of Israel is anticipated here. The new covenant addresses the root cause: "I will put my law within them, and I will write it on their hearts" (Jeremiah 31:33). What Deuteronomy 31:20 predicts as inevitable apostasy, Jeremiah 31:33 promises to cure through the inner transformation that Christ enacts through the Spirit. Theme.
21: "I know what they are already planning to do, even now, before they have entered the land I promised." YHWH's omniscient foreknowledge of human rebellion — even before the first step into the land, the failure is known. The same omniscient God who foreknew Israel's rebellion also elected before the foundation of the world: "he chose us in him before the foundation of the world" (Ephesians 1:4). Divine foreknowledge of both failure and grace coexist in the same sovereign God. Revelation of God.
22: Moses writes down the song that very day — immediate obedience to YHWH's command. The faithful servant does what the master commands on the day it is commanded. In Christ, the perfect obedience extends to every command of the Father: "I always do the things that are pleasing to him" (John 8:29); "I have glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work that you gave me to do" (John 17:4). Revelation of God.
23: "Be strong and courageous, for you will bring the Israelites into the land I swore to give them. I will be with you." YHWH's direct commissioning of Joshua with the assurance of divine presence. The "I will be with you" formula runs through the OT (Genesis 26:24; Deuteronomy 28:15; Exodus 3:12; Joshua 1:5) and reaches its ultimate expression in the Incarnation: "Emmanuel, which means God with us" (Matthew 1:23). The assurance given to every covenant servant finds its permanent form in the Son who is himself "God with us." Theme.
24: Moses finishes writing the entire law in a book. The completion of the written Torah — Moses' literary commission fulfilled. The written Torah is complete. Christ's completion of his redemptive mission echoes this: "It is finished" (John 19:30 — τετέλεσται, the perfect tense of completion). What Moses finished was a covenant document; what Christ finished was the redemption the document pointed toward. Type.
25: Moses gives the command to the Levites who carried the ark. The Levites as the carriers of the covenant — their ministry is the physical preservation and transport of YHWH's dwelling and law. In Christ, the church carries a greater testimony: "we are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us" (2 Corinthians 5:20). The covenant-carriers of the old covenant are the type of the gospel-bearers of the new. Theme.
26: "Take this scroll of the law and place it beside the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God. It will remain there as a witness against you." The book placed beside the ark as a witness against covenant violation — the Torah testifies against the lawbreaker. In Christ, the accusatory testimony is replaced by the advocating intercession: "he always lives to make intercession for them" (Hebrews 7:25). The Torah that witnesses against becomes, through Christ, the covenant that witnesses for. Shadow/contrast.
27: "I know how rebellious and stubborn you are. Even today, while I am still with you, you have been defiant toward the LORD." Moses' honest final assessment: the problem is not lack of instruction but incorrigible stubbornness. This is the OT's most direct statement that the human heart's resistance to God is congenital and persistent. The gospel addresses the same assessment: not by better law but by new birth — "you must be born again" (John 3:7) — and by the Spirit who transforms the stubborn heart from within (Ezekiel 36:26–27). Revelation of God leading to new covenant promise.
28: "Gather all the elders... so I can speak these words in their hearing and call heaven and earth to testify against them." The cosmic covenant lawsuit assembled before Moses' death. Heaven and earth as witnesses to the covenant. In Revelation, the cosmic court is assembled before the throne of Christ for the final judgment (Revelation 20:11–15). The Deuteronomic lawsuit-witnesses are the type of the eschatological judgment court. Shadow.
29: "I know that after my death you will become deeply corrupt and turn away from everything I have commanded. Disaster will come upon you in the days ahead." Moses predicts the post-exodus apostasy that Israel's entire subsequent history fulfilled — from Judges through the exile. In Christ, the cycle of apostasy that Moses predicts is broken not by human resolve but by the Spirit who writes the law on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26). The prediction of failure is the negative space that makes the new covenant's promise of transformation necessary and credible. Theme.
30: Moses recites the words of this song in the hearing of the entire assembly — the great covenant witness-poem (Deuteronomy 32) proclaimed publicly to all Israel. The covenant community hears the words that will testify against them. In Revelation 15:3–4, the redeemed sing this song before the throne: "They sang the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb." What was sung as a covenant lawsuit against Israel becomes, in the eschatological assembly, the song of the redeemed. The trajectory from accusatory witness to redemptive praise is the arc of the whole Bible. Direct NT connection (Revelation 15:3–4).
